title: The terms of trade
description: Practice what you have learned about trade in this exercise.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements is true about specialization and trade?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: A country should specialize in and export the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and export the good for which they have comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and export a good for which they have both comparative and absolute advantage

assessment_items.question_content:
Joya can make an apple pie in $20$ minutes and she can prepare a turkey in $60$ minutes.

Chris needs $25$ minutes to make a pie but requires $100$ minutes to prepare a turkey.

**Based on this information, who should focus on making turkey for Thanksgiving dinner?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Whoever has a comparative advantage in preparing turkey should prepare the turkey.

If it takes Joya 20 minutes to make a pie and 60 minutes to prepare a turkey, then every time she makes a turkey she gives up 3 pies:

$3 \times 20 \text{ minutes } = 60 \text{ minutes}$

If it takes Chris 25 minutes to make a pie and 100 minutes to prepare a turkey, then every time he makes a turkey she gives up 4 pies:

$4 \times 25 \text{ minutes } = 100 \text{ minutes}$

Joya gives up fewer pies per turkey, therefore she has a 
 comparative advantage in producing turkey.

choices.content: Chris
Joya
Neither
Both
We cannot tell

The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 8, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 8. Curve A is the outer frontier, starting at (0, 8) on the Y-axis and ending at (8, 0) on the X-axis. Country B
Neither
Both

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about specialization and trade is true?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: A country should specialize in and export the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has comparative advantage.
A country should specialize in and import the good for which it has absolute advantage.
A country should specialize in and export the good for which it has a comparative advantage.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Kim can write either $10$ articles or complete $5$ worksheets and Leah can either write $3$ articles or complete $9$ worksheets.

**If Kim and Leah specialize and trade, who should specialize in each good?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each person should specialize in the good they have a comparative advantage in. To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of articles)} \cdot A = \text{(Maximum number of worksheets)} \cdot W$

To find the opportunity cost of articles solve for A, and to find the opportunity cost of worksheets solve for W.

The equation that represents Kim's production possibilities is:

$10 A = 5  W$

We can solve for A to get the opportunity cost of articles:

$\begin{align} 10 A&=5 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{10} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{10A}{\blue{10}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{10}A}{\blue{\cancel{10}}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5}{10}W~~~~~~ \text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W\end{align}$

Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is $\dfrac{1}{2}$of a worksheet:

$A=\dfrac{1}{2}W$

To find Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{2}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{2} W \cdot \blue{2}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{2}} W \cdot \blue{\cancel{2}}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &=  W \end{align}$

Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $2$ articles:

$2A=W$

Leah's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$3 A = 9 W$

To find her opportunity cost of an article, solve for $A$:

$\begin{align} 3A&=9 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{3} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{3A}{\blue{3}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{3}A}{\blue{\cancel{3}}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9}{3}W~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=3W\end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of an article is $3$ worksheets:

$A=3W$

To find Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=3W~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by }\blue{3}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{3W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{\cancel{3}W}{\cancel{\blue{3}}} \\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &=  W\\\\
\dfrac{1}{\blue{3}}A &=  W
 \end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an article:

$\dfrac{1}{3} A=W$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing articles, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing articles.

Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is ½ of a worksheet and Leah’s opportunity cost of an article is 3 worksheets. 

Therefore, Kim has a comparative advantage in articles.

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing worksheets, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing worksheets.

Kim’s opportunity cost of a worksheet is $2$ articles and Leah’s opportunity cost of a worksheet is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an article. 

Therefore, Leah has a comparative advantage in worksheets.

The answer:

> Kim should specialize in producing articles because she has a comparative advantage in producing articles. Leah should specialize in producing worksheets because she has a comparative advantage in producing worksheets.

choices.content: Kim should specialize in writing articles, Leah should specialize in worksheets
Kim should specialize in worksheets, Leah should specialize in writing articles.
There is no opportunity for specialization because they both have an absolute advantage in producing one good.
There is no opportunity for gains from trade because neither person has a comparative advantage in producing either good.
There is no opportunity for gains from trade because both people have identical opportunity costs.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Tomer can either write $100$ lines of code or process $20$ reports, and Charlotte can either write $120$ lines of code or process $20$ reports.

**If Charlotte and Tomer specialize and trade, who should produce which good?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Each person should specialize in the good they have a comparative advantage in. To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of lines of code)} \cdot C = \text{(Maximum number of reports)} \cdot  R$

To find the opportunity cost of lines of code solve for C, and to find the opportunity cost of reports solve for R.

The equation that represents Charlotte's production possibilities is:

$120 C = 20 R$

We can solve for C to get the opportunity cost of lines of code:

$\begin{align} 120 C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{120} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{120C}{\blue{120}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{120}C}{\blue{\cancel{120}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}~~~~~~\text{simplfy the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R\end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{6}R$

To find Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{6}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{6} R \cdot \blue{6}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{6}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{6}}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &=  R \end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report is $6$ lines of code:

$6C=R$

Tomer's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$100C = 20 R$


To find their opportunity cost of a line of code, solve for $C$:

$\begin{align} 100C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{100} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{100C}{\blue{100}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{100}C}{\blue{\cancel{100}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20}{100}R~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R\end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ 
 of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{5}R$

To find Tomer's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{5}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{5} R \cdot \blue{5}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{5}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{5}}\\\\
\blue{5}C&=R \end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a report is $5$ lines of code:

$5C=R$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing lines of code, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing lines of code.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of a report. 

Therefore, Charlotte has a comparative advantage in lines of code.

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing reports, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing reports.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a report is 6 lines of codes and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a report is 5 of a line of code. 

Therefore Tomer has a comparative advantage in reports.

The answer:
> Charlotte should specialize in writing lines of code because she has a comparative advantage in writing lines of code. Tomer should specialize in reports because he has a comparative advantage in reports.

choices.content: Charlotte should specialize in writing lines of code, Tomer should specialize in reports
Charlotte should specialize in reports, Tomer should specialize in writing lines of code.
There is no opportunity for specialization because they both have an absolute advantage in producing one good.
There is no opportunity for gains from trade because neither person has a comparative advantage in producing either good.
Charlotte should specialize in writing lines of code but nobody should specialize in processing reports.

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Aanand can either build 6 models or prepare 4 experiments, and Pam can either build 9 models or prepare 6 experiments.

**Who should specialize in the production of which good?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each person should specialize in the good they have a comparative advantage in. To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.
You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of models)} \cdot M = \text{(Maximum number of experiments)} \cdot E$

To find the opportunity cost of models solve for M, and to find the opportunity cost of experiments solve for E.
The equation that represents Aanand's production possibilities is:

$6M = 4 E$

We can solve for $M$ to get the opportunity cost of models:

$\begin{align} 6M&=4E ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{6} \text{ to get } M \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{6M}{\blue{6}}&=\dfrac{4E}{\blue{6}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{6}M}{\blue{\cancel{6}}}&=\dfrac{4E}{\blue{6}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{4}{\blue{6}}E~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E\end{align}$



Aanand's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment:

$M=\dfrac{2}{3}E$
To find Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment, solve for E:

$\begin{align}M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\text{ to get E by itself}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}~~~~~\text{rearrange the right hand side of the equation to put fractions together}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}E \\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{\cancel{2}}{\cancel{3}} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{2}}}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &=  E~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
\blue{1.5}M&=E \end{align}$
Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models:

$1.5M=E$
Pam's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$9M=6E$


To find her opportunity cost of a model, solve for $M$:

$\begin{align} 9M&=6E ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{9} \text{ to get } M \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{9M}{\blue{9}}&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{9}M}{\blue{\cancel{9}}}&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{6E}{\blue{9}}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{6}{9}E~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E\end{align}$
Pam's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment:

$M=\dfrac{2}{3}E$
To find Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment, solve for E:

$\begin{align}M&=\dfrac{2}{3}E~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\text{ to get E by itself}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}~~~~~\text{rearrange the right hand side of the equation to put fractions together}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}E \\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &= \dfrac{\cancel{2}}{\cancel{3}} E \cdot \blue{\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{\cancel{2}}}\\\\
\blue{\dfrac{3}{2}}\cdot M &=  E~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
\blue{1.5}M&=E \end{align}$
Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of a model:

$\dfrac{2}{3} M=E$
To find who has a comparative advantage in producing models, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing models.
Aanand's opportunity cost of a model is $\dfrac{2}{3}$ of an experiment and Pam's opportunity cost of a model is$\dfrac{2}{3}$ experiment. 

Therefore, neither has a comparative advantage in models because they have identical opportunity costs.

To find who has a comparative advantage in preparing experiments, compare each agent's opportunity cost of preparing experiments.
Aanand's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models of and Pam's opportunity cost of an experiment is $1.5$ models.

Therefore, neither has a comparative advantage in experiments because they have identical opportunity costs.
The answer:
> There is no opportunity for gains from trade because neither person has a comparative advantage in producing either good.

choices.content: There is no opportunity for gains from trade because neither person has a comparative advantage in producing either good.
Pam should specialize in both goods
Pam should specialize in models and Aanand should specialize in experiments.
Aanand should specialize in models and Pam should specialize in experiments.
Aanand should specialize in models but nobody should specialize in processing experiments.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following situations would allow agents to gain from specialization and trade with one another?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The terms of trade are between the two agents' opportunity costs.
The agents have the same opportunity costs.
One agent has absolute advantage in both goods.
One agent has comparative advantage in both goods.
Either agent has absolute advantage in either of the goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Country A and Country B are potential trading partners who can produce boats and umbrellas, as illustrated in the PPCs below.

[[☃ image 1]]

Suppose the terms of trade are one boat for one umbrella.

**Who would want to sell boats based on these terms of trade, and who would want to buy boats based on these terms of trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts two production possibilities frontiers, labeled A and B, illustrating the trade-off between Airplanes on the vertical axis and Pizzas (millions) on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 8, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 8. Curve A is the outer frontier, starting at (0, 10) on the Y-axis and ending at (5, 0) on the X-axis. Curve B is the inner frontier, starting at (0, 5) on the Y-axis and ending at (10, 0) on the X-axis.

choices.content: Neither country would be willing to buy or sell boats given these terms of trade because the prices of these goods are not given in money.
Country A would want to buy boats, and Country B would want to sell boats.
Country A would want to sell boats, and Country B would want to buy boats.
Both countries would want to sell boats given these terms of trade, so  there will be no exchange.
Neither country would be willing to sell boats given these terms of trade because neither country has comparative advantage in either good.

assessment_items.question_content:
Country A and Country B are potential trading partners that can produce boats and umbrellas, as illustrated in the PPCs below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following exchange prices for boats would result in gains from trade for these two countries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A line graph depicts two production possibilities frontiers, labeled A and B, illustrating the trade-off between Boats on the vertical axis and Umbrellas on the horizontal axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 11 with increments of 1, and the horizontal axis ranges from 0 to 11 with increments of 1. Curve A is the inner frontier, starting at (0, 10) on the Y-axis and ending at (5, 0) on the X-axis. Curve B is the outer frontier, starting at (0, 10) on the Y-axis and ending at (8, 0) on the X-axis.
options.caption: PPC for Country A and County B

choices.content: $\dfrac{1}{4}$ umbrella per boat
$\dfrac{1}{3}$ umbrella per boat
$\dfrac{3}{5}$ umbrella per boat
One umbrella per boat
2.5 umbrellas per boat

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex and Henry are baking cookies and cupcakes. The input table below shows the amount of time it takes them to create each good.

 | Time to make cookies | Time to make cupcakes
- | - |
Henry | $1$ hour | $2$ hours
Alex  | $1$ hour | $3$ hours 

**Assuming Alex and Henry decide to specialize and trade cupcakes and cookies with one another, what would be a term of trade or exchange price that would satisfy both of them?**

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
In the time that it takes Henry to do a cupcake, he can also do $2$ cookies, so his opportunity cost of a cupcake is $2$ cookies.

In the time that it takes Alex to do a cupcake he can also do $3$ cookies, so his opportunity cost of a cupcake is $3$ cookies.
An acceptable trading price will lie between the two opportunity costs.

A cupcake should trade for between $2$ and $3$ cookies, such as $1.5$ cookies for each cupcake. Alex would "sell" cupcakes in exchange for cookies, and Henry would "sell" cookies in exchange for cupcakes.

choices.content: Any price where a cupcake costs less than $2$ cookies.
Any price where a cupcake costs between $2$ and $3$ cookies
Any price where a cupcake cost more than $3$ cookies
We cannot determine a mutually agreeable trading price
Any trading price is possible

assessment_items.question_content:
Kim and Leah work together on a sales team, and part of their job involves writing articles and filling out worksheets. Their manager doesn't care who does what as long as they are productive. 

Using the same amount of time and resources, Kim can write either $10$ articles or complete $5$ worksheets and Leah can either write $3$ articles or complete $9$ worksheets.

**If Kim and Leah specialize and trade, which of the following is a trading price they will agree to?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An agreeable trading price always falls between the two agents’ opportunity costs of a good. 
To find an agreeable trading price, first find the opportunity cost for each agent of producing each good. *Any* price that is between the agents' opportunity cost is a potential price.
You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of articles)} \cdot A = \text{(Maximum number of worksheets)} \cdot W$

To find the opportunity cost of articles solve for A, and to find the opportunity cost of worksheets solve for W.
The equation that represents Kim's production possibilities is:

$10 A = 5  W$

We can solve for A to get the opportunity cost of articles:

$\begin{align} 10 A&=5 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{10} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{10A}{\blue{10}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{10}A}{\blue{\cancel{10}}}&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5W}{\blue{10}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{5}{10}W~~~~~~ \text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W\end{align}$

Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is $\dfrac{1}{2}$of a worksheet:

$A=\dfrac{1}{2}W$
To find Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=\dfrac{1}{2}W~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{2}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{2} W \cdot \blue{2}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{2}} W \cdot \blue{\cancel{2}}\\\\
\blue{2}\cdot A &=  W \end{align}$

Kim's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $2$ articles:

$2A=W$
Leah's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$3 A = 9 W$

To find her opportunity cost of an article, solve for $A$:

$\begin{align} 3A&=9 W ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{3} \text{ to get } A \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{3A}{\blue{3}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{3}A}{\blue{\cancel{3}}}&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
A&=\dfrac{9}{3}W~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
A&=3W\end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of an article is $3$ worksheets:

$A=3W$
To find Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet, solve for W:

$\begin{align}A&=3W~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by }\blue{3}\text{ to get W by itself}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{3W}{\blue{3}}\\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &= \dfrac{\cancel{3}W}{\cancel{\blue{3}}} \\\\
\dfrac{A}{\blue{3}} &=  W\\\\
\dfrac{1}{\blue{3}}A &=  W
 \end{align}$

Leah's opportunity cost of a worksheet is $\dfrac{1}{3}$ of an article:

$\dfrac{1}{3} A=W$
Kim’s opportunity cost of an article is ½ of a worksheet and Leah’s opportunity cost of an article is 3 worksheets. Therefore Kim has a comparative advantage in articles and should specialize in producing articles. Kim will be willing to sell articles for more than ½ of a worksheet, and Leah will be willing to buy articles for anything less than 3 worksheets.
The answer:

> 1 article trades for 2 worksheets

choices.content: 1 article trades for 2 worksheets
1 article trades for 1/4 worksheets
1 article trades for 4 worksheets
1 article trades for 5 worksheets
1 article trades for 1/5 worksheets

assessment_items.question_content:
Using the same amount of time and resources, Tomer can either write $100$ lines of code or process $20$ reports, and Charlotte can either write $120$ lines of code or process $20$ reports.

**Which of these trading prices will Charlotte and Tomer agree on if they specialize and trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A trading price both people will agree to will be between the two agent’s opportunity costs. To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good.

Each person should specialize in the good they have a comparative advantage in. To determine who has a comparative advantage in each good, you must first find each person’s opportunity cost of producing each good and then compare the opportunity costs. Whoever has the lower opportunity cost of a good has a comparative advantage in producing that good.

You can express each person’s opportunity cost as an equation:

$\text{(Maximum number of lines of code)} \cdot C = \text{(Maximum number of reports)} \cdot  R$

To find the opportunity cost of lines of code solve for C, and to find the opportunity cost of reports solve for R.

The equation that represents Charlotte's production possibilities is:

$120 C = 20 R$

We can solve for C to get the opportunity cost of lines of code:

$\begin{align} 120 C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{120} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{120C}{\blue{120}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{120}C}{\blue{\cancel{120}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{120}}~~~~~~\text{simplfy the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R\end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{6}R$

To find Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{6}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{6}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{6} R \cdot \blue{6}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{6}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{6}}\\\\
\blue{6}\cdot C &=  R \end{align}$

Charlotte's opportunity cost of a report is $6$ lines of code:

$6C=R$

Tomer's production possibilities are represented by this equation:

$100C = 20 R$


To find their opportunity cost of a line of code, solve for $C$:

$\begin{align} 100C&=20 R ~~~~~~\text{divide each side by } \blue{100} \text{ to get } C \text{ by itself }\\\\
\dfrac{100C}{\blue{100}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
\dfrac{\cancel{100}C}{\blue{\cancel{100}}}&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20R}{\blue{100}}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{20}{100}R~~~~~~\text{simplify the fraction}\\\\
C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R\end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ 
 of a report:

$C=\dfrac{1}{5}R$

To find Tomer's opportunity cost of a report, solve for R:

$\begin{align}C&=\dfrac{1}{5}R~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by }\blue{5}\text{ to get R by itself}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{5} R \cdot \blue{5}\\\\
\blue{5}C &= \dfrac{1}{\cancel{5}} R \cdot \blue{\cancel{5}}\\\\
\blue{5}C&=R \end{align}$

Tomer's opportunity cost of a report is $5$ lines of code:

$5C=R$

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing lines of code, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing lines of code.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{6}$ of a report and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a line of code is $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of a report. 

Therefore, Charlotte has a comparative advantage in lines of code.

To find who has a comparative advantage in producing reports, compare each agent's opportunity cost of producing reports.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a report is 6 lines of codes and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a report is 5 of a line of code. 

Therefore Tomer has a comparative advantage in reports.

Charlotte’s opportunity cost of a line of code is 1/6 of a report and Tomer’s opportunity cost of a line of code is ⅕ of a report. Therefore Charlotte has a comparative advantage in lines of code and should specialize in writing code, and she is willing to sell if for anything more than ⅙, or 0.167, of a report. Tomer is willing to buy lines of code for anything less than his opportunity costs of ⅕, or 0.2, of a report.

The answer:
> 1 line of code trades for 0.18 of a report
